learn.title: Wave Energy Converters Technologies
learn.intro: Wave energy converters harness the motion of ocean waves to generate electricity, offering a vast untapped renewable resource. With over 70% of Earth covered in water, wave energy could supply 10% of global electricity demand by 2050. This technology reduces reliance on fossil fuels while leveraging predictable marine energy patterns.
learn.c1_text: Oscillating water columns use wave motion to compress air in a chamber, driving a turbine. Imagine a vertical pipe partially submerged—waves push air in and out, spinning blades. These systems achieve 25-40% efficiency and are deployed in Scotland and Portugal.
learn.c2_text: Point absorbers float on water surfaces, moving vertically with waves to drive hydraulic pumps. Each unit can generate 100-500 kW, enough for 50 homes annually. Challenges include storm resilience and maintenance costs, but they work in depths up to 200 meters.
learn.c3_text: India's 7,500 km coastline has wave energy potential of 18-20 MW. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy launched pilot projects in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat. By 2030, India aims to deploy 100 MW of wave energy capacity.

learn.title: Solid-State Battery Advantages
learn.intro: Solid-state batteries replace liquid electrolytes with solid materials, enhancing safety and energy density. They eliminate fire risks and enable faster charging, critical for electric vehicles and grid storage. With energy densities up to 500 Wh/kg, they could revolutionize renewable energy storage.
learn.c1_text: Solid electrolytes use ceramics, polymers, or sulfides to conduct ions. Imagine a sandwich with solid layers instead of liquid—like replacing juice with gel. These materials prevent dendrite formation, enabling 10x longer lifespan than lithium-ion batteries.
learn.c2_text: Solid-state batteries operate safely at higher voltages (up to 5V) and temperatures (-40°C to 100°C). Their energy density reaches 400-500 Wh/kg, doubling lithium-ion capacity. This allows EVs to travel 1,000 miles on a single charge.
learn.c3_text: India's Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme allocates $2.3 billion for advanced battery manufacturing. Companies like Exide Industries are partnering with global firms to develop solid-state prototypes. By 2025, India aims to produce 50 GWh of advanced batteries annually.
learn.stat3: India's PLI scheme: $2.3 billion for batteries

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels Efficiency
learn.intro: Bifacial solar panels generate electricity from both sides, capturing reflected light (albedo) to boost output by 10-30%. This technology maximizes energy yield in limited spaces, crucial for urban and agricultural settings. With declining costs, bifacial panels could become the new standard in solar installations.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels use monocrystalline or PERC cells on both faces. Imagine a mirror behind the panel—sunlight reflects onto the back, adding 15-25% more power. They work best on light-colored surfaces like sand or snow, where albedo is high.
learn.c2_text: Mounting systems elevate panels 1-2 meters to allow airflow and reflection. Tracking systems increase yield by 30-40% compared to fixed installations. Bifacial modules cost 10-15% more but offer higher long-term returns due to increased energy production.
learn.c3_text: India's Bhadla Solar Park uses bifacial panels, generating 10-15% more power than traditional systems. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy promotes bifacial tech in auctions. By 2025, 30% of India's utility-scale solar projects will adopt bifacial modules.

learn.title: Electric Vehicle Mechanics
learn.intro: Electric vehicles (EVs) use electric motors powered by batteries, eliminating tailpipe emissions. Regenerative braking recovers 10-20% of energy, extending range. Charging infrastructure is expanding globally, with fast-charging networks enabling 300-mile trips in 20 minutes. EVs are key to decarbonizing transport sectors.
learn.c1_text: EV motors use electromagnetic induction to convert electricity into motion. Imagine a magnet spinning inside copper coils—no combustion, just magnetic fields. AC induction motors deliver 85-95% efficiency, compared to 20-30% for gasoline engines.
learn.c2_text: When braking, EVs reverse motor function to generate electricity. This recovers 10-20% of kinetic energy, stored in batteries. For example, a Tesla Model 3 regains 15 kWh during city driving, adding 50 miles of range.
learn.c3_text: India plans 400,000 public EV charging stations by 2030. The FAME-II scheme allocates $1.3 billion for charging infrastructure. States like Delhi and Maharashtra lead with 5,000+ charging points installed.
learn.stat3: India's EV charging target: 400,000 stations by 2030

learn.title: Agrivoltaics Synergy
learn.intro: Agrivoltaics combines solar panels with farming, enabling dual land use. Panels provide shade, reducing water evaporation by 30% while maintaining crop yields. This approach addresses food-energy competition, especially in arid regions. It's a win-win for sustainable agriculture and renewable energy.
learn.c1_text: Solar panels installed 3-5 meters above crops allow sunlight penetration. Imagine a greenhouse with solar panels on the roof—crops grow beneath while generating power. This setup increases land productivity by 60-80% compared to single-use systems.
learn.c2_text: Panels reduce soil temperature by 5-10°C, cutting water needs by 30%. Tomatoes and peppers thrive under partial shade, with yields increasing by 10-20%. Sheep grazing under panels is another benefit, reducing mowing costs by $200/acre annually.
learn.c3_text: Gujarat's Saurashtra region hosts 50 MW of agrivoltaic projects. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy supports 200 pilot projects across states. By 2025, India aims to deploy 5 GW of agrivoltaic systems, prioritizing drought-prone areas.

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture
learn.intro: Direct air carbon capture (DAC) removes CO2 directly from the atmosphere using chemical processes. Paired with renewables, DAC can achieve negative emissions, offsetting industrial pollution. Though energy-intensive, advances in technology are reducing costs to $100/ton. It's a critical tool for achieving net-zero goals.
learn.c1_text: DAC systems use fans to pull air through chemical filters that bind CO2. Think of a giant vacuum cleaner with soda lime filters. One facility can capture 1 million tons annually—equivalent to removing 200,000 cars from roads.
learn.c2_text: DAC requires 1.5-3 GJ of energy per ton of CO2, ideally from renewables. Captured CO2 is stored underground or used in fuels. Current costs are $600/ton, but IEA projects $100/ton by 2030 with scaling and innovation.
learn.c3_text: India's National Hydrogen Mission includes DAC integration. The Department of Science and Technology funds 10 pilot projects. By 2030, India aims to capture 10 million tons of CO2 annually, focusing on industrial clusters and renewable-powered plants.
learn.stat4: India's DAC target: 10 million tons by 2030
